Thank you for making time, I know your schedule is tight, so I will be brief.
Let me be direct: at that price, the margin does not work for us.
Our work speaks for itself, let me show you why it is worth it.
It was great connecting at the fair, I would love to stay in touch.
I appreciate the warm welcome, shall we get started?
Milton, before your trip, a little advice on our etiquette here. Can I be honest?
Please. I would rather know. What should I do differently?
Our punctuality is strict. The small talk is short, then we go straight to numbers.
Understood. So I am supposed to be in my seat at nine, and I ought to state my price early.
Exactly. And directness is fine here, we read it as courtesy, not rudeness.
Good to know. Then I had better not undersell our work, I will state our value with confidence and build rapport through results.
With American buyers you should be assertive and direct, they read it as courtesy.
After the fair you are supposed to follow up fast, networking turns into rapport only if you stay in touch.
Thank you for making time, I will be brief.
Our work speaks for itself, let me show you why.
It was great connecting, I would love to stay in touch.

Hi Milton, it is Claire from Halstead. Before you fly to New York next week, let me give you a little friendly advice about how meetings run here. First, punctuality is not flexible. If the meeting starts at nine, you are supposed to be in your seat at nine, not at nine fifteen. Second, Americans get to the point fast. The small talk is short, maybe two minutes about the weather or your flight, and then we open the laptops. Do not read that as cold; it is just the rhythm. Third, you should feel free to disagree directly. If you think a price is too low, you ought to say so politely but clearly. We respect that. And one more thing: you had better not undersell yourself. Brazilians are often too modest in the room. Say what your work is worth. See you next Thursday.

Hey, it is Milton, leaving a quick voice note for my team after the New York trip. A few things we should keep in mind for the next visit. The meeting culture really is different. They start exactly on time, so we ought to arrive five minutes early, not five minutes late like at home. The small talk is much shorter than ours, so we should not feel rushed when they move straight to numbers. And here is the big lesson: I was too soft on the first price. I should have been more direct and stated our value from the start. Next time we had better walk in knowing our number and saying it with confidence. The relationship matters to them too, but they build it through results, not long lunches. Good trip overall. Let us talk Monday.
